evals.prompt: 我接手了一个 Spring Boot 2.1 的老项目，用的 MyBatis + MySQL，代码有几万行，没有文档。帮我分析这个项目，生成 AI 编程规则，让 Copilot 以后改代码能一次改对。项目根目录在当前工作区。
evals.expected_output: 应该检测到 Java + Spring Boot 2.1 + MyBatis，读取 java-spring.md 参考文档。按 L0-L7 逐层分析，采样 controller/service/mapper 文件提取模式。生成 legacy-rules.md 单文件（按维度分章节：项目概览/架构规范/命名规范/编码惯例/API设计/数据访问/安全规范/依赖约束/已知坑点/自定义规则）。规则应为指令式语气，包含从项目中提取的真实代码 DO/DON'T 示例。编码惯例章节应包含 import 别名惯例。最后进入交互式补充阶段询问口头约定和已知坑点。

evals.prompt: 这个 ThinkPHP 5.1 的电商后台项目太难维护了，500多个PHP文件，很多方法都上千行。我需要给它生成一套规则文件，这样用 Cursor 改代码时能保持和现有代码风格一致，不会引入新的问题。只分析 application/api/ 这个模块就行。
evals.expected_output: 应该检测到 PHP + ThinkPHP 5.1，读取 php-thinkphp-laravel.md。注意用户指定了模块范围（application/api/），但仍需先读全局 L0/L1 获取整体上下文。重点分析 api 模块的 controller/model/validate 文件。生成 legacy-rules.md 主规则文件 + legacy-rules-api.md 模块级规则。规则应特别关注 ThinkPHP 5.1 的路由模式、Model 查询方式、响应格式。作为大型项目（500+文件），采样策略应更聚焦。

evals.prompt: 帮我分析这个 Node.js 项目生成规则。项目用的 Express + Sequelize + MySQL，Node 10 版本，很多地方还在用回调，代码风格很混乱。我主要担心 AI 改代码时会把回调改成 async/await 或者引入新的 npm 包。
evals.expected_output: 应该检测到 Node.js + Express + Sequelize，读取 nodejs-express-koa-egg.md。关键分析点：async 模式检测（回调 vs Promise vs async/await 的比例），CommonJS vs ESM，require 别名一致性。用户明确关注点：(1) 不要把回调改成 async/await (2) 不要引入新 npm 包。生成 legacy-rules.md 单文件，编码惯例章节重点强调 async 模式规则和 import/require 别名惯例，依赖约束章节严格限制新包引入。已知坑点章节标注混合 async 模式的风险。Node 10 限制了可用语法特性（无 optional chaining 等）。

evals.prompt: I inherited a Django 2.2 project running on Python 3.7. It uses Django REST Framework for APIs and Celery for background tasks. The codebase has about 15 Django apps and no documentation. Please analyze it and generate rules so AI assistants can safely make changes.
evals.expected_output: Should detect Python + Django 2.2 + DRF, read python-django-flask.md reference. Analyze L0-L7 layers: settings structure (single settings.py or split), URL patterns, view style (FBV vs CBV vs DRF ViewSets), serializer patterns, model conventions, Celery task patterns. Generate legacy-rules.md with sections: Project Overview (Python 3.7 + Django 2.2 + DRF + Celery), Architecture (Django app structure, dependency direction between apps), Naming Conventions (model/serializer/view naming), Coding Patterns (import conventions — absolute vs relative imports, error handling, DRF response format, Celery task definition pattern), API Design (DRF router/URL conventions, pagination, authentication), Data Access (Django ORM patterns, migration approach, model field conventions), Security (DRF permission classes, authentication backends), Dependencies (pinned versions from requirements.txt), Known Pitfalls (Python 3.7 version constraints — no walrus operator, no dict union). Should enter interactive phase to gather oral conventions.

evals.prompt: We have a Go microservice built with Gin and GORM v2, running Go 1.18. It handles payment processing and is extremely sensitive — any AI-generated code must follow our exact patterns. Analyze the project and produce rules. Focus especially on error handling and transaction patterns.
evals.expected_output: Should detect Go 1.18 + Gin + GORM v2, read go-specifics.md reference. Focus areas per user request: error handling pattern detection (fmt.Errorf %w vs pkg/errors vs bare returns), transaction patterns (GORM db.Transaction callback style vs manual Begin/Commit/Rollback). Analyze L0-L7 with emphasis on L4 (business logic — error handling, logging) and L5 (data access — GORM query patterns, transaction boundaries). Generate legacy-rules.md with sections: Project Overview (Go 1.18 + Gin + GORM v2, payment domain — high risk), Architecture (project layout — cmd/internal/pkg or flat, dependency injection pattern), Naming Conventions (interface naming, constructor patterns, package naming), Coding Patterns (error handling DO/DON'T with real code, logging library and pattern, context propagation, import grouping and alias consistency), API Design (Gin handler patterns, middleware chain, response envelope), Data Access (GORM v2 query patterns, transaction management, model conventions, migration approach), Security (payment-specific security considerations, input validation, sensitive data handling), Dependencies (Go 1.18 features available — generics, but check if project uses them), Known Pitfalls (GORM v1 vs v2 API differences, concurrency patterns in payment processing). Should flag payment module as high-risk and potentially generate legacy-rules-payment.md.

evals.prompt: Analyze this .NET 6 Web API project. It uses Entity Framework Core with a Clean Architecture pattern (separate Api, Application, Domain, and Infrastructure projects). We recently migrated from .NET Core 3.1 and some old patterns still exist. Generate comprehensive rules.
evals.expected_output: Should detect .NET 6 + EF Core + Clean Architecture, read dotnet-specifics.md reference. Key analysis: detect whether the project uses minimal hosting (Program.cs only) or legacy Startup.cs pattern — since it migrated from 3.1, likely has Startup.cs still. Check for mixed patterns: old IHostingEnvironment vs new IWebHostEnvironment, AddMvc() vs AddControllers(). Analyze multi-project solution structure (Api/Application/Domain/Infrastructure). Generate legacy-rules.md with sections: Project Overview (.NET 6 + EF Core, Clean Architecture, migration history from 3.1), Architecture (layer boundaries — Domain has no external dependencies, Application references Domain only, Infrastructure implements interfaces from Application, Api references all), Naming Conventions (controller/service/repository/entity naming, interface conventions like IUserRepository), Coding Patterns (DI registration patterns — AddScoped/AddTransient/AddSingleton, async/await patterns, error handling — ProblemDetails or custom middleware, logging with ILogger<T> or Serilog, using directive consistency — check for global usings), API Design (controller patterns with [ApiController], route conventions, response wrapping, Swagger attributes), Data Access (EF Core patterns — Fluent API vs Data Annotations, repository pattern usage, Unit of Work, migration strategy, query patterns — LINQ vs raw SQL), Security (JWT Bearer or Identity, [Authorize] attribute placement, policy-based authorization), Dependencies (EF Core version, Newtonsoft.Json vs System.Text.Json), Known Pitfalls (migration artifacts from 3.1, mixed old/new patterns, C# 10 features available but may not be used everywhere). Should enter interactive phase asking about migration-related quirks.
